title: Getting infrastructure costs right: The tool that could save governments billions
gist: World Bank economists unveil R2C2, a free web tool built on a global road-and-rail cost dataset that isolates how terrain, rainfall, and urban density drive construction costs, replacing the unreliable national-average estimates that routinely cause government infrastructure budgets to overrun.

insights.claim: Cost overruns are routine in transport infrastructure worldwide largely because governments lack reliable tools to estimate what a road or railway should cost before committing to build it, and hiring engineers to study every potential project is too expensive and slow.
insights.speaker: Stéphane Straub and He He
insights.so_what: Frames chronic infrastructure cost overruns as fundamentally a data/tooling gap rather than a corruption or design problem — a fixable bottleneck.
insights.quote: governments do not have reliable tools to estimate what a road or railway should cost before they commit to building it.
insights.locator: Cost overruns are not an anomaly in infrastructure

insights.claim: In the absence of engineering studies, transport agencies fall back on broad national average costs or outdated databases like the Road Costs Knowledge System (ROCKS), even though a road through mountainous terrain costs far more than one across flat savanna, and urban routes cost far more than rural ones.
insights.speaker: Stéphane Straub and He He
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific legacy tool (ROCKS) and practice that R2C2 is designed to displace.
insights.locator: Hiring engineers to study every potential project is too expensive

insights.claim: A new global dataset of road and rail construction costs across many landscapes and economies, published in the 'Infrastructure Foundations: From Current Assets to Future Growth' report, isolates how much mountains, annual rainfall, and urban build-up drive up costs, and finds a massive dispersion in unit costs across projects.
insights.speaker: Stéphane Straub and He He
insights.so_what: The underlying dataset is the evidentiary basis for the R2C2 tool and quantifies just how unreliable flat national averages are as a budgeting input.
insights.quote: the dispersion in unit costs across road and rail projects is massive.
insights.locator: To understand how much local conditions affect costs

insights.claim: The Road & Rail Cost Calculator (R2C2) is a web app that lets planners draw a proposed route on a map and, combined with user-entered details like lane count or tunneling needs, generates a tailored, context-specific cost estimate in seconds without requiring specialist engineering expertise.
insights.speaker: Stéphane Straub and He He
insights.so_what: Lowers the cost and skill barrier for infrastructure cost estimation, particularly valuable for lower-capacity transport ministries in developing economies.
insights.quote: The result is a tailored cost estimate in seconds, built on the same research that underpins the Infrastructure Foundations report.
insights.locator: To see how this works in practice

insights.claim: For a proposed 16-kilometer road in Tanzania, R2C2 replaces the old practice of applying a flat national average cost per kilometer — which typically gets the estimate wrong by a wide margin — with a route-specific estimate that factors in terrain and built-up area.
insights.speaker: Stéphane Straub and He He
insights.so_what: A concrete worked example showing the tool's practical output, useful for gauging how it would perform on a real project appraisal.
insights.quote: a planner would apply a national average cost per kilometer and move on, likely getting the estimate wrong by a wide margin.
insights.locator: consider a transport ministry looking at a proposed 16-kilometer road in Tanzania

insights.claim: Beyond upfront project costing, R2C2 is positioned for comparing dozens of route options before funding full feasibility studies, letting finance ministries value entire road and rail networks as balance-sheet assets, estimating climate-risk replacement costs for financing or insurance decisions, and speeding reconstruction-cost estimates in post-conflict or post-disaster settings.
insights.speaker: Stéphane Straub and He He
insights.so_what: Extends the tool's relevance beyond project appraisal into sovereign balance-sheet management and climate/disaster finance — multiple potential government-procurement or donor-funded use cases to track.
insights.locator: This kind of fast, context-specific estimation

insights.claim: The tool's release is framed as timely given tighter government budgets and higher borrowing costs, which the authors argue make simply spending more on infrastructure and hoping for growth no longer a viable strategy, shifting the imperative toward value-for-money cost discipline.
insights.speaker: Stéphane Straub and He He
insights.so_what: Positions rigorous cost estimation as a fiscal-discipline tool for governments facing tighter financing conditions, relevant to sovereign debt and infrastructure-financing analysis.
insights.quote: Simply spending more on infrastructure and hoping for growth is no longer a viable strategy.
insights.locator: The R2C2 app arrives at a critical moment
